tldr: Headlines across 10 countries and 3 languages name Trump’s phone call as the central fact, framing FIFA’s compliance as political capitulation, rule-bending, or institutional scandal; the headline register is sharper and more adversarial toward both FIFA and Trump than most underlying body text.

evidence.quote: Trump threatens America’s World Cup goodwill
FIFA Gives Trump Exactly What He Wants
Anruf von Trump bei FIFA löst WM-Skandal aus
Injerencia de Trump, “punta del iceberg del deterioro
Outrage as FIFA’s Infantino bends rules to Trump’s
FIFA defends Balogun ruling despite concerns
Belgium appeals against FIFA’s Folarin Balogun ban
FIFA boss referred to Olympics ethics board
European lawmakers call for probe into Infantino
tested the limits of rules-based order

silences.note: Headline 'FIFA or "Animal Farm"' uses literary allusion without signaling a codebook frame from the title alone.

single_outlet_findings.finding: 'FIFA or "Animal Farm"' is the only headline using literary allusion rather than naming Trump or FIFA directly — the most ideologically coded register in the corpus, framing American power over sport as systemic corruption.
'FIFA Gives Trump Exactly What He Wants' is the most assertively transactional headline — casting FIFA as agent of Trump’s will rather than as a governance body that failed.

'Belgium appeals against FIFA’s Folarin Balogun ban decision' names no actor’s guilt — the only headline treating the ruling as a procedural matter without editorial condemnation of Trump or Infantino.

bottom_line: The headline layer across 10 countries converges on a single editorial verdict — Trump intervened and FIFA complied — with charged language ('outrage', 'bends rules', 'exactly what he wants') that is sharper than most body text beneath, leaving iran_state’s literary headline as the sole title that resists codebook classification.
